message.content:
Adversaries may exploit non-standard ports to communicate using protocols and port pairings that are not typically associated, such as HTTPS over port 8088 or HTTP over port 14146. This technique helps them bypass network filtering and complicate traffic analysis. Various threat actors, including APT-C-36, APT32, and FIN7, have utilized non-standard ports for command and control (C2) communications. Commonly exploited ports include 443, 8080, 9005, and 9999, among others. The impact of this technique includes evasion of detection mechanisms and prolonged unauthorized access to compromised systems.

Mitigation strategies involve implementing strict network segmentation and monitoring, enforcing the use of standard ports for specific protocols, and employing intrusion detection systems (IDS) to flag unusual port activity. Regularly updating firewall rules and conducting network traffic analysis can also help identify and block non-standard port usage.
